justification: All pass criteria met. (1) Scheduling lag metric gap identified as primary SLO gap — called out as CRITICAL with specific metric name renewal_scheduling_lag_seconds histogram and noted 'This is the only way to measure the 99.9% within-1-hour SLO.' (2) Retry tracking gap explicitly identified — CRITICAL: No retry_attempts metric, cannot measure 24-hour retry SLO, entire retry pipeline called 'a black box.' (3) Current alert critiqued as symptom-based — described as 'a lagging indicator that fires after damage is done, with no leading indicators' and explicitly contrasted API-level metrics vs business-level SLO adherence. (4) Specific metric recommendations provided: renewal_scheduling_lag_seconds histogram, retry_attempt_total counter, renewal_slo_breach_total counter, renewals_pending gauge, retry_queue_depth gauge. (5) SLO-based alerting recommended — direct SLO breach counter alerting and threshold-based alerting on scheduling lag percentiles (p95 > 30min = 50% of SLO budget), though burn rate terminology not used explicitly. (6) Verdict: NOT READY.

observations.text: Exceptionally thorough output — goes well beyond minimum pass criteria with detailed failure mode analysis, on-call readiness assessment, and escalation routing
Correctly identifies the core insight: existing metrics measure API health, not business SLO compliance — 'zero metrics to measure compliance with either' SLO
Strong critique of the health endpoint as shallow — only checks billing API connectivity, not scheduler liveness or consumer health
Identifies the logged-but-not-metrified gap: scheduled_time vs processed_time is in logs but never promoted to a metric, making real-time alerting impossible
Practical on-call framing: lists 4 essential questions an on-call engineer cannot answer with current monitoring

Does not explicitly recommend burn rate alerting or rolling window success rate recording rules — the SLO-based alerting recommendation uses threshold/breach counters instead of the more standard burn rate approach
Goes beyond the rubric with dead-letter queue recommendation, circuit breaker analysis, and rollback idempotency concerns — demonstrating deep operational maturity

trials.justification: All pass criteria met: (1) Scheduling lag metric gap identified as primary SLO gap — called out as CRITICAL with specific recommendation for renewal_scheduling_lag_seconds histogram. (2) Retry tracking gap identified — CRITICAL callout for missing renewal_retry_attempts counter and renewal_retry_exhausted_total, explicitly tied to 24-hour retry SLO being unmonitored. (3) Current alert critiqued as symptom-based — stated 'existing metrics measure API behavior but not business SLO compliance' and enumerated five failure modes the single error-count alert misses. (4) Specific metric recommendations provided: renewal_scheduling_lag_seconds histogram, renewal_retry_attempts_total counter, renewal_queue_depth gauge, renewal_retry_exhausted_total counter, plus dead-man's switch. (5) SLO-based alerting recommended — mentions 'renewal_slo_compliance gauge (percentage of renewals processed within 1 hour over a rolling window)' and SLO compliance percentage alerting category. (6) Verdict is NOT READY.

observations.text: Exceptionally thorough — goes well beyond rubric requirements with six detailed failure mode scenarios, each with specific handling status and recommendations
Correctly frames the core problem: 'The two business SLOs that define success for this service are completely unmonitored'
Identifies the dead-man's switch gap for scheduler liveness — a subtle failure mode where renewals_processed_total simply stops incrementing with no alert
Strong operational framing: 'An on-call engineer at 3am' perspective grounds the analysis in real incident response needs
Identifies additional gaps beyond rubric scope: circuit breakers, timeout configuration, rollback idempotency concerns, distributed tracing absence
Escalations are well-structured with clear routing and specific reasoning tied to business impact

SLO-based alerting recommendation could be more explicit about burn rate methodology — mentions rolling window compliance gauge but does not name burn rate alerting or multi-window approach specifically
Revenue impact metric (renewal_amount_failed_total) is a nice addition but risks scope creep in a monitoring gap analysis focused on SLO compliance

trials.justification: All pass criteria met. (1) Scheduling lag metric gap identified as primary SLO gap — called out as 'MISSING: renewal_scheduling_lag_seconds (histogram)' and labeled 'the SLO metric' in metrics gaps, with the component interaction flagging 'No visibility into scheduling lag' as the top risk. (2) Retry tracking gap identified extensively — retry mechanism called 'a complete observability black hole' with specific missing metrics (retry_attempts_total, retry_exhausted_total) and a dedicated escalation. (3) Current alert critiqued as symptom-based — described as 'a blunt instrument that catches acute failures but misses slow degradation,' with the specific example that it 'fires the same for 10 declined cards (normal) and 10 gateway timeouts (critical).' (4) Specific metric recommendations provided: renewal_scheduling_lag_seconds histogram, retry_attempts_total counter with labels, retry_exhausted_total counter, renewal_queue_depth gauge, renewals_due_total as SLO denominator, renewal_slo_violations_total counter. (5) SLO-based alerting recommended via renewals_due_total (denominator for 99.9% success rate calculation) and renewal_slo_violations_total for direct SLO breach alerting — though explicit 'burn rate' terminology is absent, the mechanics are present. (6) Verdict is NOT READY.

observations.text: Exceptionally thorough analysis — goes well beyond minimum pass criteria with queue depth monitoring, circuit breaker gaps, dead-man's switch recommendation, and detailed failure mode scenarios
Strong framing of the core problem: 'metrics answer what happened but not are we healthy' and 'cannot answer are we meeting our SLOs right now — which is the only question that matters'
Excellent on-call debugging narrative: 'I know something broke, but I cannot tell how bad it is or whether it is getting worse' — makes the gap tangible
Retry gap escalation is outstanding — connects unobservable retries to 'permanent revenue loss' and notes 'retry failures are silent by nature — nobody notices until the customer churns'
Correctly identifies the current alert produces both false positives (10 declined cards) and false negatives (silent scheduling delays) — a nuanced symptom-alert critique
Provides the missing denominator insight (renewals_due_total) — without knowing how many renewals were expected, the 99.9% success rate literally cannot be computed

Does not use explicit 'burn rate alert' or 'rolling window success rate' terminology — the SLO-based alerting recommendation is present in substance (SLO violation counter + denominator for rate calculation) but not in the standard SRE vocabulary
